✳

bpm feed — Superlike modal

14 juillet 2026 · claude-fable-5 · 23 images
H
Hugo
/grill-the-interface
arguments
I created two mockups to simulate the page I want to display when a user likes one card of another profile. The goal is to display this page as a full-screen modal (with no tab bar visible) when a user clicks the Like button on one of a profile's cards. Analyze these mockups. Your goal is to perfectly implement the scenario where the user has 0 Superlikes left. Since this is a technical test, I want this use case to be implemented entirely on the client side.

This full-screen modal (or page, you choose) must be displayed before the Like animation. Since we are implementing the "0 Superlikes left" scenario, consider that the "Obtenir Superlike" button is disabled.
✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

I need to adjust my approach here since this is actually a React Native/Expo project, not a Next.js app with shadcn and Tailwind. The user is testing a dating app feed interface with mockups showing a full-screen "like" action page for a user named Marie Joséphine, with different superlike counts and button states across the variations.

For the zero-superlike scenario, I need to explore the codebase first — checking the UI conventions, feed feature structure, color system, existing like animations, and how expo-router is set up. Then I'll start interviewing with one focused question at a time, offering recommended answers as a design partner. The key decision is whether to use an Expo Router modal route with presentation: 'fullScreenModal' to hide the tab bar and get native transitions, or a standard RN Modal component — the router approach feels more natural for this app. Before recommending, I need to check the mockups against the actual design system, verify that colors.ts has the red gradient for the 0-superlike variant, and confirm all the French copy strings match what's shown.

I'm also thinking through the implementation questions: where the superlike count comes from (likely a zustand store field seeded at 0), whether the message input is truly functional or just local state that gets discarded, and what exactly happens when the user taps "Envoyer Like" — presumably the modal dismisses and triggers the like animation while advancing the feed.

For the disabled state of "Obtenir Superlike", I need to check if it's just red text at reduced opacity, and "annuler" should simply close the modal without changing the feed. The gradient will need expo-linear-gradient, and I should verify whether the red color exists in the palette or needs to be added. There's also a question about which photo displays in the modal — if the liked card isn't a photo card, should it show the profile's main photo instead? And I need to consider keyboard behavior for the text input.

For the animation flow, the like overlay animation already exists from a previous issue, so the sequence is: tap Like → modal opens → "Envoyer Like" → modal closes → play like animation → advance feed. Since the user provided mockups as inspiration rather than needing me to create them, I should focus on clarifying ambiguities and pressure-testing the different states before handing off the design.

The main question is about the disabled state for the 0 superlikes button — the mockup shows "Obtenir Superlike" in red text, but should the disabled version look identical (red but non-interactive) or should it be visually dimmed? I'll start by exploring the codebase, then ask this one question at a time with a recommended answer.

I'll start by reading the actual codebase — the feed feature, colors, existing like flow, and any UI conventions — so my questions build on what's really there.

1	// Palette from docs/brief.md, plus mockup-eyedropped additions (approximate),
2	// flagged as liberties in the README. The only place hex values may live.
3	export const COLORS = {
4	  surface: '#0D0D0D',
5	  panel: '#1A1A1A',
6	  fill: '#FFFFFF',
7	  fillOpposite: '#0D0D0D',
8	  strokeDefault: 'rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.05)',
9	  strokeStrong: 'rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.1)',
10	  primary: '#E1FF00',
11	  boost: '#48C1F3',
12	  superlike: '#FF5252',
13	  accentPurple: '#B36BFF',
14	  accentPink: '#FF2D87',
15	  textMuted: '#9A9A9A',
16	} as const;

✳Réflexion

I've confirmed the color palette has the red and lime I need, so now I'm reviewing the key files to understand the current structure — the like button component, feed screen, store setup, constants, reaction overlay, app layouts, the feed types, and font configuration.

⛭4 actions · Read
Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/features/feed/components/feed-screen.tsx
1	import { ActivityIndicator, StyleSheet, Text, View } from 'react-native';
2	import { useSafeAreaInsets } from 'react-native-safe-area-context';
3	
4	import { useFeed } from '@/features/feed/api';
5	import { FeedEmptyState } from '@/features/feed/components/feed-empty-state';
6	import { PassButton } from '@/features/feed/components/pass-button';
7	import { ReactionOverlay } from '@/features/feed/components/reaction-overlay';
8	import { FEED_ERROR_MESSAGE } from '@/features/feed/constants';
9	import { isFeedFinished, useFeedStore } from '@/features/feed/store';
10	import { COLORS } from '@/utils/colors';
11	import { FeedHeader } from './feed-header';
12	import { ProfileCardList } from './profile-card-list';
13	
14	export function FeedScreen() {
15	  const insets = useSafeAreaInsets();
16	  const { data, isPending } = useFeed();
17	  const currentProfileIndex = useFeedStore(
18	    (state) => state.currentProfileIndex,
19	  );
20	  const reaction = useFeedStore((state) => state.reaction);
21	  const likeCard = useFeedStore((state) => state.likeCard);
22	  const passProfile = useFeedStore((state) => state.passProfile);
23	  const advance = useFeedStore((state) => state.advance);
24	  const restart = useFeedStore((state) => state.restart);
25	
26	  const profiles = data?.profiles ?? [];
27	  const currentProfile = profiles.at(currentProfileIndex);
28	  const finished =
29	    isFeedFinished(currentProfileIndex, profiles.length) && profiles.length > 0;
30	
31	  // The overlay renders in every branch: the last profile's reaction must
32	  // fade out onto the empty state, not unmount with the feed.
33	  return (
34	    <View style={[styles.container, { paddingTop: insets.top }]}>
35	      {isPending ? (
36	        <View style={styles.centered}>
37	          <ActivityIndicator color={COLORS.fill} size="large" />
38	        </View>
39	      ) : finished ? (
40	        <View style={styles.centered}>
41	          <FeedEmptyState onRestart={restart} />
42	        </View>
43	      ) : !currentProfile ? (
44	        <View style={styles.centered}>
45	          <Text style={styles.errorText}>{FEED_ERROR_MESSAGE}</Text>
46	        </View>
47	      ) : (
48	        <>
49	          <FeedHeader firstname={currentProfile.firstname} />
50	          {/* Keyed remount on profile change resets the scroll to the top. */}
51	          <ProfileCardList
52	            cards={currentProfile.cards}
53	            key={currentProfile.userId}
54	            onLikeCard={likeCard}
55	          />
56	          <PassButton onPress={() => passProfile(currentProfile.userId)} />
57	        </>
58	      )}
59	      {/* Advancing happens mid-overlay, once it is fully opaque. */}
60	      <ReactionOverlay onSwap={advance} reaction={reaction} />
61	    </View>
62	  );
63	}
64	
65	const styles = StyleSheet.create({
66	  container: {
67	    flex: 1,
68	    backgroundColor: COLORS.surface,
69	  },
70	  centered: {
71	    flex: 1,
72	    alignItems: 'center',
73	    justifyContent: 'center',
74	  },
75	  errorText: {
76	    color: COLORS.textMuted,
77	    fontSize: 15,
78	  },
79	});
80
Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/features/feed/store.ts
1	import { create } from 'zustand';
2	
3	// Transient reaction: set by likeCard/passProfile, cleared by advance. The
4	// reaction overlay animates it between those two moments.
5	export type FeedReaction = 'like' | 'pass';
6	
7	type FeedStore = {
8	  currentProfileIndex: number;
9	  likedCardIds: string[];
10	  passedProfileIds: string[];
11	  reaction: FeedReaction | null;
12	  likeCard: (cardId: string) => void;
13	  passProfile: (profileId: string) => void;
14	  advance: () => void;
15	  restart: () => void;
16	};
17	
18	const INITIAL_STATE = {
19	  currentProfileIndex: 0,
20	  likedCardIds: [],
21	  passedProfileIds: [],
22	  reaction: null,
23	} satisfies Omit<FeedStore, 'likeCard' | 'passProfile' | 'advance' | 'restart'>;
24	
25	// Genuine client state only (feed progression) — the profiles themselves stay
26	// in TanStack Query. In-memory by design: no persistence.
27	export const useFeedStore = create<FeedStore>()((set) => ({
28	  ...INITIAL_STATE,
29	  // Both reactions are no-ops while one is already pending: a rapid
30	  // double-tap must record and advance exactly one profile.
31	  likeCard: (cardId) =>
32	    set((state) =>
33	      state.reaction
34	        ? state
35	        : {
36	            likedCardIds: [...state.likedCardIds, cardId],
37	            reaction: 'like',
38	          },
39	    ),
40	  passProfile: (profileId) =>
41	    set((state) =>
42	      state.reaction
43	        ? state
44	        : {
45	            passedProfileIds: [...state.passedProfileIds, profileId],
46	            reaction: 'pass',
47	          },
48	    ),
49	  advance: () =>
50	    set((state) => ({
51	      currentProfileIndex: state.currentProfileIndex + 1,
52	      reaction: null,
53	    })),
54	  restart: () => set(INITIAL_STATE),
55	}));
56	
57	// Finished = index past the last profile.
58	export function isFeedFinished(
59	  currentProfileIndex: number,
60	  profileCount: number,
61	): boolean {
62	  return currentProfileIndex >= profileCount;
63	}
64
Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/features/feed/constants.ts
1	import type { RelationshipType, TrainingFrequency } from '@/types/feed';
2	
3	// All user-facing copy is French. bpm mixes French and franglais on purpose:
4	// "Likes", "Matchs", "Boost" and "Rewind" are the app's own terms.
5	export const TAB_LABELS = {
6	  HOME: 'Accueil',
7	  LIKES: 'Likes',
8	  MATCHES: 'Matchs',
9	  PROFILE: 'Profil',
10	} as const;
11	
12	// Used as the pushed placeholder pages' titles and as the header buttons' labels.
13	export const HEADER_ACTION_TITLES = {
14	  FILTERS: 'Filtres',
15	  REWIND: 'Rewind',
16	  BOOST: 'Boost',
17	} as const;
18	
19	export const BACK_LABEL = 'Retour';
20	
21	// Deliberately static (README liberty): incoming likes and remaining boosts
22	// are not modeled by the feed data.
23	export const LIKES_TAB_BADGE = '4';
24	export const BOOST_BADGE = '1';
25	
26	// Minimal error state: shown when the feed can't load (or comes back empty).
27	export const FEED_ERROR_MESSAGE = 'Impossible de charger les profils.';
28	
29	export const HEIGHT_UNIT = 'cm';
30	
31	// Info card "looking for" copy. "Endurance" is the real app's wording for
32	// exclusive; the casual/intimate running metaphors are invented (README liberty).
33	export const RELATIONSHIP_TYPE_COPY: Record<
34	  RelationshipType,
35	  { label: string; tagline: string }
36	> = {
37	  exclusive: { label: 'Endurance', tagline: 'Le jeu long, sans chrono' },
38	  casual: { label: 'Fractionné', tagline: 'À fond, puis on souffle' },
39	  intimate: { label: 'Sprint', tagline: 'Intense, sans détour' },
40	};
41	
42	// Photo Allowance input: how many photos the viewer has uploaded. Static by
43	// design — the viewer's own profile is not modeled. With the fixture (max 2
44	// pictures per profile) 3 never demotes; lower to 1 to see demotion live.
45	export const VIEWER_UPLOADED_PHOTOS_COUNT = 3;
46	
47	// Locked picture card copy. The CTA is inert: adding photos is out of scope.
48	export const LOCKED_PICTURE_COPY = {
49	  TITLE: 'Tu vois autant de photos que tu en partages.',
50	  SUBTITLE: 'Débloque plus de photos en ajoutant les tiennes.',
51	  CTA: 'Ajouter mes photos',
52	} as const;
53	
54	// Feed interaction copy. Like/Pass buttons are icon-only: these label them
55	// for accessibility. The block & report pill is rendered but inert (user
56	// decision: blocking/reporting is out of scope).
57	export const LIKE_LABEL = 'Liker';
58	export const PASS_LABEL = 'Passer';
59	export const BLOCK_REPORT_LABEL = 'Bloquer & Signaler';
60	
61	// Reaction overlay sequence: opaque fade-in, hold with the popping icon while
62	// the profile swap happens invisibly underneath, fade-out onto the next one.
63	export const REACTION_FADE_IN_MS = 200;
64	export const REACTION_HOLD_MS = 1000;
65	export const REACTION_FADE_OUT_MS = 250;
66	
67	// Empty state once every profile got a reaction. The restart button is a demo
68	// affordance (user-approved liberty): it replays the same feed from the start.
69	export const EMPTY_FEED_MESSAGE = 'Plus de profils pour le moment';
70	export const RESTART_LABEL = 'Revoir les profils';
71	
72	// Sport card list header (rendered uppercase, as in the mockup).
73	export const SPORT_CARD_LABEL = 'Séances / semaine';
74	
75	// Frequency → displayed sessions-per-week value.
76	export const TRAINING_FREQUENCY_VALUE: Record<TrainingFrequency, string> = {
77	  little: '1-2',
78	  mid: '3-4',
79	  hard: '5+',
80	};
81	
82	// Frequency → ring sweep, as a fraction of the full circle (user-approved
83	// rule: the ring fill is prop
Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/features/feed/components/like-button.tsx
1	import { Heart } from 'lucide-react-native';
2	import { Pressable, StyleSheet } from 'react-native';
3	
4	import { LIKE_LABEL } from '@/features/feed/constants';
5	import { COLORS } from '@/utils/colors';
6	
7	export type LikeButtonProps = {
8	  onPress: () => void;
9	  // Overlay (default): absolutely pinned to the parent's bottom-right corner
10	  // (picture cards). Otherwise the button sits in normal flow and the parent
11	  // card positions it (text cards place it inside the panel, bottom-right).
12	  overlay?: boolean;
13	};
14	
15	export function LikeButton({ onPress, overlay = true }: LikeButtonProps) {
16	  return (
17	    <Pressable
18	      accessibilityLabel={LIKE_LABEL}
19	      accessibilityRole="button"
20	      onPress={onPress}
21	      style={overlay ? styles.overlayButton : styles.button}
22	    >
23	      <Heart color={COLORS.fillOpposite} size={22} />
24	    </Pressable>
25	  );
26	}
27	
28	const baseButton = {
29	  width: 48,
30	  height: 48,
31	  borderRadius: 24,
32	  alignItems: 'center',
33	  justifyContent: 'center',
34	  backgroundColor: COLORS.fill,
35	} as const;
36	
37	const styles = StyleSheet.create({
38	  button: baseButton,
39	  overlayButton: {
40	    ...baseButton,
41	    position: 'absolute',
42	    bottom: 14,
43	    right: 14,
44	  },
45	});

Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/app/_layout.tsx
1	import { QueryClientProvider } from '@tanstack/react-query';
2	import { useFonts } from 'expo-font';
3	import { Stack } from 'expo-router';
4	import * as SplashScreen from 'expo-splash-screen';
5	import { StatusBar } from 'expo-status-bar';
6	import * as SystemUI from 'expo-system-ui';
7	import { useEffect } from 'react';
8	
9	import { queryClient } from '@/lib/query-client';
10	import { COLORS } from '@/utils/colors';
11	import { TITLE_FONT_FAMILY } from '@/utils/fonts';
12	
13	SplashScreen.preventAutoHideAsync();
14	SystemUI.setBackgroundColorAsync(COLORS.surface);
15	
16	export default function RootLayout() {
17	  const [fontsLoaded] = useFonts({
18	    [TITLE_FONT_FAMILY]: require('../assets/fonts/cal-sans-regular.ttf'),
19	  });
20	
21	  useEffect(() => {
22	    if (fontsLoaded) {
23	      SplashScreen.hideAsync();
24	    }
25	  }, [fontsLoaded]);
26	
27	  if (!fontsLoaded) {
28	    return null;
29	  }
30	
31	  return (
32	    <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>
33	      <StatusBar style="light" />
34	      <Stack
35	        screenOptions={{
36	          headerShown: false,
37	          contentStyle: { backgroundColor: COLORS.surface },
38	        }}
39	      />
40	    </QueryClientProvider>
41	  );
42	}
43
Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/types/feed.ts
1	// Types for the profile feed API response.
2	// See docs/feed-response-example.json for a sample payload.
3	
4	// ============ Shared vocabularies ============
5	
6	export type Gender = 'woman' | 'man';
7	
8	export type TrainingFrequency = 'little' | 'mid' | 'hard';
9	
10	export type PromptCategory = 'lifestyle' | 'sport';
11	
12	export type RelationshipType = 'exclusive' | 'casual' | 'intimate';
13	
14	export type Zodiac =
15	  | 'aries'
16	  | 'taurus'
17	  | 'gemini'
18	  | 'cancer'
19	  | 'leo'
20	  | 'virgo'
21	  | 'libra'
22	  | 'scorpio'
23	  | 'sagittarius'
24	  | 'capricorn'
25	  | 'aquarius'
26	  | 'pisces';
27	
28	export type Diet =
29	  | 'omnivore'
30	  | 'flexitarian'
31	  | 'vegetarian'
32	  | 'pescatarian'
33	  | 'vegan';
34	
35	// Frequency-style habits: the sample payload only shows a subset of values,
36	// they likely share a fuller scale (never / sometimes / socially / often)
37	export type DrinkingHabit = 'never' | 'socially' | 'often';
38	
39	export type SmokingHabit = 'never' | 'sometimes';
40	
41	export type DrugsHabit = 'never';
42	
43	export type KidsIntention =
44	  | 'want'
45	  | 'dont_want'
46	  | 'unsure'
47	  | 'have'
48	  | 'have_want_more'
49	  | 'dont_have';
50	
51	export type PetsStatus = 'dog' | 'cat' | 'other' | 'none' | 'want';
52	
53	export type Religion =
54	  | 'agnostic'
55	  | 'atheist'
56	  | 'spiritual'
57	  | 'christian'
58	  | 'muslim'
59	  | 'buddhist'
60	  | 'other';
61	
62	// ============ Card contents ============
63	
64	export interface PictureContent {
65	  storageKey: string;
66	  imageUrl: string;
67	  thumbhash: string;
68	  promptKey: string | null; // a picture can optionally answer a prompt
69	  promptTitle: string | null;
70	}
71	
72	export interface PromptAnswerContent {
73	  promptKey: string;
74	  promptTitle: string;
75	  answerText: string;
76	  category: PromptCategory;
77	}
78	
79	export interface SportPractice {
80	  sportKey: string; // open vocabulary ("climbing", "padel", ...)
81	  sportName: string; // localized display name
82	  sportIcon: string; // icon identifier ("mountain", "bike", ...)
83	  trainingFrequency: TrainingFrequency;
84	}
85	
86	export interface SportCardContent {
87	  sports: SportPractice[];
88	  trainingFrequency: TrainingFrequency; // overall frequency across sports
89	}
90	
91	export interface InfoCardContent {
92	  age: number;
93	  height: number; // cm
94	  city: string;
95	  relationshipType: RelationshipType;
96	  zodiac: Zodiac;
97	  diet: Diet;
98	  drinking: DrinkingHabit;
99	  smoking: SmokingHabit;
100	  drugs: DrugsHabit;
101	  kids: KidsIntention;
102	  pets: PetsStatus;
103	  religion: Religion;
104	  job: string;
105	  education: string | null;
106	  originCity: string;
107	}
108	
109	// Blurred/teaser picture: no storageKey or imageUrl on purpose
110	export interface LockedPictureContent {
111	  thumbhash: string;
112	  promptTitle: string | null;
113	}
114	
115	// ============ Cards (discriminated union) ============
116	
117	interface BaseCard {
118	  id: string; // UUID
119	  position: number; // display order within the profile
120	}
121	
122	export interface PictureCard extends BaseCard {
123	  type: 'picture';
124	  content: PictureContent;
125	}
126	
127	export interface PromptAnswerCard extends BaseCard {
128	  type: 'prompt_answer';
129	  content: PromptAnswerContent;
130	}
131	
132	export interface SportCard extends BaseCard {
133	  type: 'sport_card';
134	  content: SportCardContent;
135	}
136	
137	export interfa
Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/features/feed/components/profile-card-list.tsx
1	import { ScrollView, StyleSheet, View } from 'react-native';
2	
3	import { BlockReportPill } from '@/features/feed/components/block-report-pill';
4	import { InfoCard } from '@/features/feed/components/info-card';
5	import { LikeButton } from '@/features/feed/components/like-button';
6	import { LockedPictureCard } from '@/features/feed/components/locked-picture-card';
7	import { PictureCard } from '@/features/feed/components/picture-card';
8	import { PromptAnswerCard } from '@/features/feed/components/prompt-answer-card';
9	import { SportCard } from '@/features/feed/components/sport-card';
10	import { VIEWER_UPLOADED_PHOTOS_COUNT } from '@/features/feed/constants';
11	import { applyPhotoAllowance } from '@/features/feed/photo-allowance';
12	import type { ProfileCard } from '@/types/feed';
13	
14	export type ProfileCardListProps = {
15	  cards: ProfileCard[];
16	  onLikeCard: (cardId: string) => void;
17	};
18	
19	// Likable Cards (see CONTEXT.md): unlocked pictures, prompt answers, sport
20	// cards. Info cards never are; locked pictures aren't either ("you can't
21	// like what you can't see").
22	function isLikable(card: ProfileCard, locked: boolean): boolean {
23	  if (locked) {
24	    return false;
25	  }
26	  return (
27	    card.type === 'picture' ||
28	    card.type === 'prompt_answer' ||
29	    card.type === 'sport_card'
30	  );
31	}
32	
33	// Text cards (sport, prompt answer) render the like button inside their own
34	// panel, so the list only overlays a button on the remaining Likable Cards.
35	function hasInternalLike(card: ProfileCard): boolean {
36	  return card.type === 'prompt_answer' || card.type === 'sport_card';
37	}
38	
39	function renderCard(card: ProfileCard, locked: boolean, onLike: () => void) {
40	  switch (card.type) {
41	    case 'picture':
42	      return locked ? (
43	        <LockedPictureCard
44	          imageUrl={card.content.imageUrl}
45	          promptTitle={card.content.promptTitle}
46	          thumbhash={card.content.thumbhash}
47	        />
48	      ) : (
49	        <PictureCard content={card.content} />
50	      );
51	    case 'prompt_answer':
52	      return <PromptAnswerCard content={card.content} onLike={onLike} />;
53	    case 'sport_card':
54	      return <SportCard content={card.content} onLike={onLike} />;
55	    case 'info_card':
56	      return <InfoCard content={card.content} />;
57	    case 'locked_picture':
58	      return (
59	        <LockedPictureCard
60	          promptTitle={card.content.promptTitle}
61	          thumbhash={card.content.thumbhash}
62	        />
63	      );
64	    default:
65	      // Unreachable — every card kind is handled; biome can't tell.
66	      return null;
67	  }
68	}
69	
70	export function ProfileCardList({ cards, onLikeCard }: ProfileCardListProps) {
71	  const orderedCards = [...cards].sort((a, b) => a.position - b.position);
72	  const allowedCards = applyPhotoAllowance(
73	    orderedCards,
74	    VIEWER_UPLOADED_PHOTOS_COUNT,
75	  );
76	
77	  return (
78	    <ScrollView
79	      contentContainerStyle={styles.content}
80	      showsVerticalScrollIndicator={false}
81	      style={styles.scroll}
82	    >
83	      {allowedCards.map(({ card, locked }) => (
84	        <View key={card.id}>
85	          {renderCard(card, locked, () => onLikeCard(card.id))}
86	          {isLikable(card, locked) && !hasInternalLike(card) ? (
87	            <LikeButton onPress={() => onLikeCard(card.id)} />
88	          ) : null}
89	        </View>
90	      ))}
91	      <Bloc

Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/features/feed/components/reaction-overlay.tsx
1	import { Heart, X } from 'lucide-react-native';
2	import { useEffect, useState } from 'react';
3	import { StyleSheet } from 'react-native';
4	import Animated, {
5	  runOnJS,
6	  useAnimatedStyle,
7	  useSharedValue,
8	  withDelay,
9	  withSequence,
10	  withSpring,
11	  withTiming,
12	} from 'react-native-reanimated';
13	
14	import {
15	  REACTION_FADE_IN_MS,
16	  REACTION_FADE_OUT_MS,
17	  REACTION_HOLD_MS,
18	} from '@/features/feed/constants';
19	import type { FeedReaction } from '@/features/feed/store';
20	import { COLORS } from '@/utils/colors';
21	
22	export type ReactionOverlayProps = {
23	  reaction: FeedReaction | null;
24	  // Fired once the overlay is fully opaque: the swap happens invisibly.
25	  onSwap: () => void;
26	};
27	
28	const ICON_SIZE = 96;
29	const ICON_ENTRY_SCALE = 0.5;
30	
31	export function ReactionOverlay({ reaction, onSwap }: ReactionOverlayProps) {
32	  // Latched copy: the store clears `reaction` at the swap (mid-sequence), but
33	  // the overlay must keep covering the feed until the fade-out completes.
34	  const [activeReaction, setActiveReaction] = useState<FeedReaction | null>(
35	    null,
36	  );
37	  const opacity = useSharedValue(0);
38	  const iconScale = useSharedValue(1);
39	
40	  useEffect(() => {
41	    if (!reaction) {
42	      return;
43	    }
44	    setActiveReaction(reaction);
45	    opacity.set(0);
46	    iconScale.set(ICON_ENTRY_SCALE);
47	    iconScale.set(withSpring(1));
48	    opacity.set(
49	      withSequence(
50	        withTiming(1, { duration: REACTION_FADE_IN_MS }, (finished) => {
51	          if (finished) {
52	            runOnJS(onSwap)();
53	          }
54	        }),
55	        withDelay(
56	          REACTION_HOLD_MS,
57	          withTiming(0, { duration: REACTION_FADE_OUT_MS }, (finished) => {
58	            if (finished) {
59	              runOnJS(setActiveReaction)(null);
60	            }
61	          }),
62	        ),
63	      ),
64	    );
65	  }, [reaction, opacity, iconScale, onSwap]);
66	
67	  const overlayStyle = useAnimatedStyle(() => ({
68	    opacity: opacity.get(),
69	  }));
70	  const iconStyle = useAnimatedStyle(() => ({
71	    transform: [{ scale: iconScale.get() }],
72	  }));
73	
74	  if (!activeReaction) {
75	    return null;
76	  }
77	
78	  // While mounted the overlay covers the feed and swallows every touch, so no
79	  // reaction input can fire until the sequence is over.
80	  return (
81	    <Animated.View style={[styles.overlay, overlayStyle]}>
82	      <Animated.View style={iconStyle}>
83	        {activeReaction === 'like' ? (
84	          <Heart
85	            color={COLORS.primary}
86	            fill={COLORS.primary}
87	            size={ICON_SIZE}
88	          />
89	        ) : (
90	          <X color={COLORS.fill} size={ICON_SIZE} strokeWidth={2.5} />
91	        )}
92	      </Animated.View>
93	    </Animated.View>
94	  );
95	}
96	
97	const styles = StyleSheet.create({
98	  overlay: {
99	    position: 'absolute',
100	    top: 0,
101	    left: 0,
102	    right: 0,
103	    bottom: 0,
104	    alignItems: 'center',
105	    justifyContent: 'center',
106	    backgroundColor: COLORS.surface,
107	  },
108	});
109
Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/features/feed/components/picture-card.tsx
1	import { Image } from 'expo-image';
2	import { StyleSheet, View } from 'react-native';
3	
4	import { PromptHeader } from '@/features/feed/components/prompt-header';
5	import type { PictureContent } from '@/types/feed';
6	import { COLORS } from '@/utils/colors';
7	
8	export type PictureCardProps = {
9	  content: PictureContent;
10	};
11	
12	export function PictureCard({ content }: PictureCardProps) {
13	  const { promptTitle } = content;
14	  const answersPrompt = promptTitle !== null;
15	
16	  const photo = (
17	    <Image
18	      contentFit="cover"
19	      placeholder={{ thumbhash: content.thumbhash }}
20	      placeholderContentFit="cover"
21	      source={{ uri: content.imageUrl }}
22	      style={answersPrompt ? styles.promptPhoto : styles.barePhoto}
23	      transition={300}
24	    />
25	  );
26	
27	  if (!answersPrompt) {
28	    return photo;
29	  }
30	
31	  return (
32	    <View style={styles.panel}>
33	      <PromptHeader title={promptTitle} />
34	      {photo}
35	    </View>
36	  );
37	}
38	
39	const styles = StyleSheet.create({
40	  barePhoto: {
41	    width: '100%',
42	    aspectRatio: 3 / 4,
43	    borderRadius: 24,
44	  },
45	  panel: {
46	    backgroundColor: COLORS.panel,
47	    borderRadius: 24,
48	    // The photo bleeds to the panel edges; the panel radius clips its corners.
49	    overflow: 'hidden',
50	    borderWidth: 1,
51	    borderColor: COLORS.strokeDefault,
52	  },
53	  promptPhoto: {
54	    width: '100%',
55	    aspectRatio: 3 / 4,
56	  },
57	});
58

{
  "name": "bpm",
  "main": "expo-router/entry",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "expo start",
    "android": "expo start --android",
    "ios": "expo start --ios",
    "web": "expo start --web",
    "lint": "biome check --write .",
    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit",
    "test": "vitest run",
    "knip": "knip",
    "check": "npm run lint && npm run typecheck && npm run test && npm run knip"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@tanstack/react-query": "^5.101.2",
    "expo": "~57.0.4",
    "expo-font": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-image": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-linking": "~57.0.2",
    "expo-router": "~57.0.4",
    "expo-splash-screen": "~57.0.2",
    "expo-status-bar": "~57.0.0",
    "expo-system-ui": "~57.0.0",
    "lucide-react-native": "^1.24.0",
    "react": "19.2.3",
    "react-dom": "19.2.3",
    "react-native": "0.86.0",
    "react-native-gesture-handler": "~2.32.0",
    "react-native-reanimated": "4.5.0",
    "react-native-safe-area-context": "~5.7.0",
    "react-native-screens": "4.25.2",
    "react-native-svg": "15.15.4",
    "react-native-web": "~0.21.0",
    "react-native-worklets": "0.10.0",
    "zustand": "^5.0.14"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@biomejs/biome": "^2.5.3",
    "@types/react": "~19.2.2",
    "knip": "^6.26.0",
    "react-native-svg-transformer": "^1.5.3",
    "typescript": "~6.0.3",
    "vitest": "^4.1.10"
  },
  "private": true
}
import { PlaceholderScreen } from '@/components/placeholder-screen';
import { HEADER_ACTION_TITLES } from '@/features/feed/constants';

export default function BoostRoute() {
  return <PlaceholderScreen showBack title={HEADER_ACTION_TITLES.BOOST} />;
}
✳Réflexion

I'm mapping out the architecture: a fullScreenModal route for the like action would naturally hide the tab bar, and I have the color palette to work with—superlike in red and primary in lime. Since there's no linear gradient dependency available, I'll need to work with what's built into React Native.

The flow shifts from an immediate like action to opening a modal showing the profile's photo and name with an "Envoyer Like" button, then triggering the like after dismissal. For the superlike count, I'll use a static constant since we're only handling the zero-scenario without mutations, which aligns with how the codebase handles other static values.

On the photo selection — if the user likes a prompt or sport card where the profile has no picture, I need a fallback like thumbhash or panel color, which is worth clarifying. The message input stays optional and client-side only, discarded on send, with KeyboardAvoidingView to keep buttons visible. For the "Obtenir Superlike" button when disabled, I'll render it exactly as the mockup shows — red text with red heart, non-interactive with proper accessibility state, no dimming or press feedback since the mockup itself represents the zero-superlike design.

I'm approaching this systematically: layout and hierarchy first, then components, then states, then refinements.

Starting with the biggest structural question — whether to use an expo-router full-screen modal route or a native RN Modal component. The expo-router approach with presentation: 'fullScreenModal' makes more sense: it hides the tab bar, gets native iOS slide-up animation, handles Android back properly, and follows the existing pattern for boost/filters/rewind. I'd add the modal presentation and slide-from-bottom animation to the route options. The tricky part is passing data — I need cardId and userId as route params, then fetch firstname, photo URL, thumbhash, and superlike count from the userId.

The alternative is rendering a RN Modal inside FeedScreen with local state, which is simpler for data flow but less idiomatic for an expo-router app. The router route is more aligned with what the user asked for and enables deep-linking. The sequencing works like this: when the user taps "Envoyer Like", I call router.back() to dismiss the modal, then trigger the like action with the cardId — the like animation plays after the modal closes. The flow is straightforward: send triggers setModalClosed which then calls likeCard. For the implementation, I'm leaning toward using the router's fullScreenModal route since it avoids the tab bar, gives that native slide-up feel, and fits the expo-router pattern of keeping route files thin while housing the actual feature logic in a dedicated component. Now I need to decide on a couple of structural details: whether to use the router approach versus a React Native Modal, and which photo should display in the header—either the liked card's photo if it's a picture card, or fall back to the profile's first unlocked picture, with a thumbhash as the final fallback. I think showing the specific photo they liked makes more sense for a dating app — it's clearer feedback. For the gradient, I'm using red from the superlike color, and I'll go with expo-linear-gradient since it's purpose-built and works well with Expo Go, though that's more of an implementation detail than something that affects the user experience.

For the superlike count, I'm deciding between a static constant in constants.ts or a zustand store field. The constant approach is consistent with how we handle other static values like the likes tab badge, and there's no mutation logic for it anyway, so I'll recommend that — though it's worth flagging to Hugo since it touches on client state architecture and testability.

On the message input, I'm leaning toward a multiline TextInput with local state only, and the text just gets discarded without any fake mutations since the API handles the actual submission.

Now I'm walking through the states and destinations: the cancel chip dismisses the modal without recording anything, and for the superlike button when disabled, I need to decide between rendering it as the mockup shows (full red but non-functional) or dimming it visually to signal it's disabled. The mockup already displays the zero-superlike state in full red, but from an affordance perspective that could mislead users into thinking it's tappable.

I'm leaning toward rendering it exactly as the mockup shows with the disabled prop and accessibility state set, then flagging the affordance concern as something worth discussing. For the like action, the key question is timing: should the like animation trigger when the sheet starts dismissing (so it plays as the overlay slides down) or wait until dismissal completes? I think triggering them simultaneously creates a smoother, more cohesive motion.

On the technical side, the modal needs to handle Android back gestures and iOS swipe-down dismiss properly — fullScreenModal doesn't support swiping by default, so I should clarify which presentation style works best. Also confirming that the like flow intercepts every likable card type globally, and checking the title typography against the mockup to see if it's using that chunky font.

For the red variant specifically, the title should be white using the fill color, with the subtitle also white but possibly at reduced opacity, and the heart icon in a lighter pinkish tone. The buttons need careful styling — "Envoyer Like" as a white filled pill matching the existing LikeButton style, and "Obtenir Superlike" as text-only.

The "annuler" chip sits top-right as a dark translucent pill with white lowercase text, while the photo card itself is a full-bleed rounded element with a gradient overlay that transitions from transparent to the background color, starting around halfway down and becoming solid at the bottom where the title and subtitle sit. Below the photo is the message input area.

I'm planning to ask one question at a time following the skill guidelines, and I'll start by decomposing the mockup design choices and confirming them before moving into the flow. For the color mapping, I'm translating the mockup's visual elements into the design system—the black background becomes the surface color, the red gradient maps to the superlike color, white buttons use the fill color with opposite text, and the placeholder text uses the muted text color.

Now I'm ready to write out the introductory analysis of the mockups and present the first question about the container approach.

I've read the feed feature end-to-end — the like flow (store.ts, like-button.tsx, reaction-overlay.tsx), the router shell, and the palette. There's no docs/ui-conventions.md yet. Before I start grilling, here's my read of your mockups translated into this codebase's vocabulary — tell me if I've misread anything as we go.

Decomposition of the "0 Superlikes" mockup (the one we're building):

Element Designer term RN build Codebase mapping
Photo block Hero media card with scrim expo-image + gradient overlay, radius ~32 photo from the liked profile, COLORS.superlike (#FF5252 — already in the palette) fading up from the bottom
"annuler" Dismiss chip (pill button, top-right, overlaying the hero) Pressable pill, dark bg COLORS.panel bg, white lowercase label
Title + count Overlay title block Cal Sans title (TITLE_FONT_FAMILY), system subtitle + filled heart "Envoyer un like à {firstname}" / "Il vous reste 0 Superlikes"
Message field Underlined ghost input TextInput with hairline bottom border placeholder COLORS.textMuted, rule COLORS.strokeStrong
"Obtenir Superlike ❤" Text button (ghost), disabled Pressable disabled red text + filled heart, COLORS.superlike
"Envoyer Like" Primary filled pill Pressable pill ~56px COLORS.fill bg, COLORS.fillOpposite label — same recipe as the existing like button

Flow as I understand it: tapping the like button on any Likable Card no longer calls likeCard directly — it opens this screen first. "Envoyer Like" dismisses it and then the existing reaction overlay + advance plays. "annuler" dismisses with the feed untouched. Everything client-side; the lime "12 Superlikes" variant is out of scope (we only build the red 0-state).

One dependency note: the gradient needs expo-linear-gradient (Expo Go-compatible, npx expo install) — react-native-svg could fake it but the dedicated package is cleaner.

Now, top of the decision tree — the container, since everything else hangs off it:

I'm wondering whether the liked card should display the specific photo that was liked or always default to the profile's first picture.

Every fixture profile leads with a picture card, so a "first photo" always exists. Now the hierarchy question:

⛭1 action · AskUserQuestion
AskUserQuestion
Your questions have been answered: "Which photo fills the hero when the screen opens? You can like three kinds of cards — pictures, prompt answers, sport cards — and the last two have no photo of their own."="Always the card the user liked, it can be a picture but also other type of cards". You can now continue with these answers in mind.
✳Réflexion

The user wants the hero to display whatever card they liked—so if it's a prompt answer or sport card, those render directly in the hero instead of a separate picture card. That opens up some design considerations: picture cards could use a full-bleed photo with a red gradient and title overlay, but for the darker text-based cards, I need to figure out how they'd scale and appear in that hero space.

I'm weighing whether to keep a consistent hero frame (maintaining the 3/4 aspect ratio with the red gradient and title overlay) and fit the card inside it, or let the hero height vary depending on the card type. The tricky part is that overlaying a title on top of a dark text card might obscure the answer content, so maybe the title sits below the card for text cards instead—though that breaks visual consistency. Option A keeps everything consistent—one hero frame with the same red scrim and title treatment for all card types, which matches the mockup exactly. The tricky part is handling the like button: text cards (PromptAnswerCard and SportCard) have their own like button logic, but inside the hero we don't want it showing. I could pass a prop to hide it, which fits the pattern of adding props only when the current implementation needs them. The key decision to lock in is whether text cards get the uniform framed treatment or render at natural size with the title below.

✳Réflexion

I've identified that the hero element is the liked card itself, which means text cards need different treatment since they're dark panels with their own content rather than photo-based designs. I'll now work through how to reconcile this with the existing title and scrim treatment.

⛭1 action · AskUserQuestion
AskUserQuestion
Your questions have been answered: "When the liked card is a prompt answer or sport card (a dark text panel, not a photo), how does the hero compose with the red gradient + "Envoyer un like à…" title?"="One uniform frame (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.
✳Réflexion

I'm working through the superlike count implementation, deciding whether to store it as a constant in the constants file or in a zustand store. The constant approach seems cleaner since there's no mutation happening and it mirrors how the likes tab badge is handled statically. After that, I need to handle the component-level disabled state for the "Obtenir Superlike" button, then work through the sequencing for sending likes, message input behavior with keyboard handling, and the cancel and back navigation flows.

Uniform frame locked. Next — where "0" lives, since it's the one piece of data that isn't in the feed response:

⛭1 action · AskUserQuestion
AskUserQuestion
Your questions have been answered: "Where does the Superlike count (the "0" in "Il vous reste 0 Superlikes") come from? feed.ts doesn't model it and must stay untouched, so it's viewer-side client data either way."="Constant in constants.ts (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.
✳Réflexion

I'm weighing the design decision for the disabled "Obtenir Superlike" button — the mockup shows it in full red, which visually suggests it's enabled, but the user says it should be disabled. The tension here is between following the mockup exactly as presented versus applying proper affordance principles where disabled controls should appear dimmed or inactive. I need to clarify whether this red state is meant to be the actual disabled appearance or if it's showing what the button looks like when superlikes are available, since in the real app it would be enabled at zero superlikes to open the paywall.

Looking at the codebase, there's already a precedent with the locked-picture flow where CTAs are rendered as designed but remain functionally inert — that's the pattern I should follow here. So I'd recommend keeping the button exactly as the mockup shows it (red, full color) but marking it as disabled in the accessibility layer and making it non-interactive, treating this as a scope cut where the paywall flow isn't implemented. This respects the mockup as the source of truth while being clear about the functional constraint.

✳Réflexion

I'm confirming the constant matches the existing badge permissions, and now examining the disabled "Obtenir Superlike" component where design and usability considerations intersect.

⛭1 action · AskUserQuestion
AskUserQuestion
Your questions have been answered: "How should the disabled "Obtenir Superlike ❤" text button look? The mockup paints it full-strength red — which normally signals "tappable" — but in our scope it does nothing."="As mockup: red, inert (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.
✳Réflexion

I'm weighing two approaches for the "Envoyer Like" interaction: triggering the dismiss and like card simultaneously so the reaction overlay fades in while the modal slides down, versus dismissing first and waiting for the transition to complete before showing the like. The simultaneous approach seems cleaner since both animations can run in parallel.

I should also note that the lime heart overlay uses the existing primary color animation, so both options already satisfy the requirement of showing the modal before the like animation. For the cancel button, router.back() handles it without recording anything, and Android's hardware back button gets that behavior for free — though iOS swipe-down dismissal isn't enabled by default on fullScreenModal.

Now I'm moving on to the message input details and keyboard behavior, then I'll wrap up with a recap and conventions. Since the user provided mockups, I could generate an HTML mockup file showing the initial state with the exact palette and a states strip including the text-card hero variant and keyboard interactions — that would add clarity on the composition.

Now the destination walk for the primary action — the sequencing you can actually see:

⛭1 action · AskUserQuestion
AskUserQuestion
Your questions have been answered: "When "Envoyer Like" is tapped, how do the modal dismissal and the existing like animation (lime heart overlay → advance to next profile) sequence?"="Dismiss + animate together (Recommended)". You can now continue with these answers in mind.
✳Réflexion

I'm thinking through the message input behavior—specifically whether it should be single-line or multiline, how typed text gets handled without persisting to the API, and how the keyboard interacts with the bottom buttons. The mockup shows a subtle underline with a placeholder like "Un petit message personnalisé (optionnel)" and plenty of whitespace below, suggesting the input should feel spacious and optional.

Given that dating app messages are typically short (a couple lines at most), I'm leaning toward a multiline TextInput with a reasonable character limit around 280, wrapped in KeyboardAvoidingView so the send button lifts above the keyboard rather than getting hidden. This way the user can type and tap send directly without dismissing the keyboard first—better flow overall.

The message content stays local to component state and never persists anywhere; it's discarded on both send and cancel, so the input is purely functional for this interaction.

✳Réflexion

I'm pinning down one last behavior: the message field and keyboard, which hold local state that gets discarded on send or cancel rather than being attached to a fake mutation per the API seam rule.

⛭1 action · AskUserQuestion
AskUserQuestion
Your questions have been answered: "How does the message field behave when the keyboard opens? The bottom of the screen holds the two CTAs, which an open keyboard would cover."="Static layout, dismiss to send". You can now continue with these answers in mind.
✳Réflexion

The user went with a static layout and single-line input where return dismisses the dialog, keeping things minimal and aligned with Hugo's design approach. Before handing this off, I should create a mockup to get visual approval—and while the user provided mockups already, there are some compositions they didn't cover, like how the text-card hero with the prompt answer or sport card would look within the uniform frame. That's genuinely new territory worth confirming visually, so I'm going to build out a mockup file to iterate on.

I'm setting up a self-contained HTML mockup that matches the app's aesthetic—black surface, Cal Sans typography. I can reference the font file via a relative path from the mockups folder and include a fallback, which should work fine when opened in a browser. I'll pull in real-ish photos from the fixture's image URLs and lay out both the main 0-superlike state with the photo hero and a states strip showing the text-card hero variation.

I need to check the .gitignore to confirm the mockups folder is ignored, then grab the fixture URLs and examine the actual component files—prompt-answer-card, sport-card, and prompt-header—to match their styling in the mockup. I'll also account for the phone frame presentation with a dark background, safe area insets, and status bar styling.

✳Réflexion

All layout decisions are finalized. I'll now build a versioned HTML mockup to confirm how a text card displays within the red hero frame, using real card designs and fixture content.

⛭3 actions · Read, Bash
Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/features/feed/components/prompt-answer-card.tsx
1	import { Quote } from 'lucide-react-native';
2	import { StyleSheet, Text, View } from 'react-native';
3	
4	import { LikeButton } from '@/features/feed/components/like-button';
5	import type { PromptAnswerContent } from '@/types/feed';
6	import { COLORS } from '@/utils/colors';
7	import { TITLE_FONT_FAMILY } from '@/utils/fonts';
8	
9	export type PromptAnswerCardProps = {
10	  content: PromptAnswerContent;
11	  onLike: () => void;
12	};
13	
14	// content.category (lifestyle/sport) is deliberately not rendered (README note).
15	export function PromptAnswerCard({ content, onLike }: PromptAnswerCardProps) {
16	  return (
17	    <View style={styles.panel}>
18	      <View style={styles.header}>
19	        {/* Same "99" quote glyph as picture prompt headers in the real app;
20	            lucide Quote filled white is the shared substitution (no asset). */}
21	        <View style={styles.badge}>
22	          <Quote color={COLORS.fill} fill={COLORS.fill} size={14} />
23	        </View>
24	        <Text style={styles.title}>{content.promptTitle}</Text>
25	      </View>
26	      <Text style={styles.answer}>{content.answerText}</Text>
27	      <View style={styles.likeRow}>
28	        <LikeButton onPress={onLike} overlay={false} />
29	      </View>
30	    </View>
31	  );
32	}
33	
34	const styles = StyleSheet.create({
35	  panel: {
36	    borderWidth: StyleSheet.hairlineWidth * 2,
37	    borderColor: COLORS.strokeDefault,
38	    backgroundColor: COLORS.panel,
39	    borderRadius: 24,
40	    padding: 20,
41	    paddingTop: 24,
42	    paddingBottom: 20,
43	    gap: 20,
44	  },
45	  likeRow: {
46	    alignItems: 'flex-end',
47	  },
48	  header: {
49	    flexDirection: 'row',
50	    alignItems: 'center',
51	    gap: 12,
52	  },
53	  badge: {
54	    width: 32,
55	    height: 32,
56	    borderRadius: 12,
57	    alignItems: 'center',
58	    justifyContent: 'center',
59	    backgroundColor: COLORS.strokeStrong,
60	  },
61	  title: {
62	    flex: 1,
63	    color: COLORS.textMuted,
64	    fontSize: 11,
65	    fontWeight: '600',
66	    textTransform: 'uppercase',
67	  },
68	  answer: {
69	    color: COLORS.fill,
70	    fontFamily: TITLE_FONT_FAMILY,
71	    fontSize: 23,
72	  },
73	});

1	import { Quote } from 'lucide-react-native';
2	import { StyleSheet, Text, View } from 'react-native';
3	
4	import { COLORS } from '@/utils/colors';
5	
6	export type PromptHeaderProps = {
7	  title: string;
8	};
9	
10	// Header row shared by picture and locked-picture cards answering a prompt.
11	export function PromptHeader({ title }: PromptHeaderProps) {
12	  return (
13	    <View style={styles.header}>
14	      {/* Lucide Quote filled white: substitution for bpm's "99" quote glyph (no asset). */}
15	      <View style={styles.badge}>
16	        <Quote color={COLORS.fill} fill={COLORS.fill} size={14} />
17	      </View>
18	      <Text style={styles.title}>{title}</Text>
19	    </View>
20	  );
21	}
22	
23	const styles = StyleSheet.create({
24	  header: {
25	    flexDirection: 'row',
26	    alignItems: 'center',
27	    gap: 10,
28	    padding: 8,
29	  },
30	  badge: {
31	    width: 32,
32	    height: 32,
33	    borderRadius: 12,
34	    alignItems: 'center',
35	    justifyContent: 'center',
36	    backgroundColor: COLORS.strokeStrong,
37	  },
38	  title: {
39	    flex: 1,
40	    color: COLORS.fill,
41	    fontSize: 16,
42	    fontWeight: '600',
43	  },
44	});

✳Réflexion

I'm noticing that .grilled/ isn't in the gitignore file, but the skill documentation says it should be—yet the project has .grilled/issues/ already committed with recent changes tracked in git history, which seems contradictory to what the guidelines suggest.

Now I'm building the mockup HTML for the send-like interface. The phone frame is roughly 390×844 pixels with a dark background (#0D0D0D), and I'm working out the proportions: the hero image should take up about the top 63% of the screen with a 3:4 aspect ratio to match the card design. For the border radius, the mockup suggests something rounder than the standard 24px used elsewhere—I'm leaning toward 32px for the hero to match that visual weight. I'm also starting to define the scrim gradient that fades from the bottom. Input field with muted placeholder text and a subtle white underline, then a centered "Obtenir Superlike" button in red with a filled heart icon (disabled state), followed by a white pill-shaped "Envoyer Like" button with dark text and proper spacing accounting for the home indicator. Below that I'm mapping out different component states, starting with a prompt-answer hero variant that uses a 3:4 frame with the panel background and top-aligned content. Pressed states will show opacity changes for annuler and Envoyer, with Obtenir Superlike disabled and unresponsive — I'll keep this compact as a small reference table rather than full screens. The mockup itself will include the main photo hero, two variant heroes for the prompt and sport card, a keyboard-open panel showing how buttons get covered, and that states reference. I'm loading Cal Sans via @font-face from the local assets, using the unsplash fixture URL for the photo, and I'll inline simple SVGs for the heart, quote, and X icons instead of relying on external dependencies.

✳Réflexion

I'm keeping .grilled/issues/ committed since it's the project's issue tracker, so mockups will live there alongside the issues. Now I'll grab the sport card's design and write the mockup.

Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/src/features/feed/components/sport-card.tsx
1	import { StyleSheet, Text, View } from 'react-native';
2	import Svg, { Line } from 'react-native-svg';
3	
4	import { ActivityRings } from '@/features/feed/components/activity-rings';
5	import { LikeButton } from '@/features/feed/components/like-button';
6	import {
7	  SPORT_CARD_LABEL,
8	  TRAINING_FREQUENCY_SWEEP,
9	  TRAINING_FREQUENCY_VALUE,
10	} from '@/features/feed/constants';
11	import type { SportCardContent } from '@/types/feed';
12	import { COLORS } from '@/utils/colors';
13	
14	export type SportCardProps = {
15	  content: SportCardContent;
16	  onLike: () => void;
17	};
18	
19	// Accent shared by a row's value and its ring, by position (outermost = first).
20	const SPORT_ACCENTS = [COLORS.primary, COLORS.accentPurple, COLORS.accentPink];
21	
22	// content.trainingFrequency (overall) and sportIcon are deliberately not
23	// rendered — neither appears in the mockups (README note).
24	export function SportCard({ content, onLike }: SportCardProps) {
25	  return (
26	    <View style={styles.panel}>
27	      <View style={styles.body}>
28	        <ActivityRings
29	          rings={content.sports.map((sport, index) => ({
30	            color: SPORT_ACCENTS[index % SPORT_ACCENTS.length],
31	            sweep: TRAINING_FREQUENCY_SWEEP[sport.trainingFrequency],
32	          }))}
33	        />
34	        <View style={styles.list}>
35	          <Text style={styles.label}>{SPORT_CARD_LABEL}</Text>
36	          {content.sports.map((sport, index) => (
37	            <View key={sport.sportKey} style={styles.row}>
38	              <Text numberOfLines={1} style={styles.sportName}>
39	                {sport.sportName}
40	              </Text>
41	              {/* SVG dotted leader: single-side dotted borders don't render on Android. */}
42	              <Svg height={2} style={styles.leader}>
43	                <Line
44	                  stroke={COLORS.textMuted}
45	                  strokeDasharray="1 6"
46	                  strokeLinecap="round"
47	                  strokeOpacity={0.5}
48	                  strokeWidth={2}
49	                  x1="0"
50	                  x2="100%"
51	                  y1="1"
52	                  y2="1"
53	                />
54	              </Svg>
55	              <Text
56	                style={[
57	                  styles.value,
58	                  { color: SPORT_ACCENTS[index % SPORT_ACCENTS.length] },
59	                ]}
60	              >
61	                {TRAINING_FREQUENCY_VALUE[sport.trainingFrequency]}
62	              </Text>
63	            </View>
64	          ))}
65	        </View>
66	      </View>
67	      <View style={styles.likeRow}>
68	        <LikeButton onPress={onLike} overlay={false} />
69	      </View>
70	    </View>
71	  );
72	}
73	
74	const styles = StyleSheet.create({
75	  panel: {
76	    borderWidth: StyleSheet.hairlineWidth * 2,
77	    borderColor: COLORS.strokeDefault,
78	    backgroundColor: COLORS.panel,
79	    borderRadius: 24,
80	    padding: 20,
81	    paddingTop: 24,
82	    paddingBottom: 20,
83	    gap: 16,
84	  },
85	  body: {
86	    flexDirection: 'row',
87	    alignItems: 'center',
88	    gap: 16,
89	  },
90	  likeRow: {
91	    alignItems: 'flex-end',
92	  },
93	  list: {
94	    flex: 1,
95	    gap: 6,
96	  },
97	  label: {
98	    color: COLORS.textMuted,
99	    fontSize: 13,
100	    fontWeight: '600',
101	    letterSpacing: 1,
102	    textTransform: 'uppercase',
103	  },
104	  row: {
105	    flexDirection: 'row',
106	    alignItems: 'center',
107	    gap: 10,
108	  }
✳Réflexion

I'm setting up the HTML mockup with a dark background to make the phone frames stand out, then laying out a header with the title and a row of device frames showing the main screen with a hero photo and content matching the user's design. Writing SVG icons... Writing component layout... For the title inside the hero, I'm using CalSans at 32px with tight line-height and a 17px system font subtitle with a heart icon. The prompt hero variant has a dark panel background with a badge and uppercase muted title at the top, then the answer text in CalSans, with a red scrim and title overlay. The sport hero variant uses a similar panel layout but with SVG rings and a list showing dotted leaders with colored values, also with the scrim and title treatment. I need to fetch the Camille sport content next.

The scroll position is at 1840 with a viewport of 1280x720 and document height of 2560, putting us near the bottom — though the screenshot came back black, possibly because it captured during the scroll animation or hit the gap between wrapped rows. With the viewport width, two phone variants fit per row with the third wrapping, so four total variants should display as two rows of two.

I'm working through the layout math—with flex-wrap enabled and items that don't have explicit widths, I need to figure out why the second row isn't visible at that scroll position. Two variants should fit per row given the container width, but I'm recalculating whether three might actually fit instead.

The real issue is that everything below y≈900 appears black in the screenshot, which suggests a rendering problem rather than a layout issue. The images from Unsplash might be blocking the paint, or the headless capture isn't repainting the scrolled content properly. I should verify the actual DOM structure and element positions using JavaScript to check the offsetTop values of each variant and see what's actually there.

✳Réflexion

The collisions are resolved and the mockup aligns with the design. I'll keep the temp server running in the background so you can view it directly in the pane. Now I'll recap what we've built and ask if the mockup matches your vision before we document the conventions. I should wrap up by presenting the full recap and decisions to the user for approval rather than proceeding silently. I'll use AskUserQuestion with options to approve or request tweaks, and I'll flag the design choices I made—like the hero radius of 32 instead of 24, which is more rounded than typical card styling—so they can weigh in. The modal route will be src/app/send-like, and I've noted the expo-linear-gradient dependency as an implementation detail.

The collisions are fixed — both text-card heroes now clear the "annuler" chip. v1 is ready for your eyes (it's open in the Browser pane, served at http://127.0.0.1:8788/.grilled/mockups/send-like-v1.html — I left the little static server running so the Cal Sans font loads; the file itself lives at send-like-v1.html).

Recap of the interface we've settled, component by component:

  • Container — expo-router fullScreenModal route (thin src/app/send-like.tsx, screen in features/feed/components/send-like-screen.tsx), slide-from-bottom, tab bar hidden for free. Params userId + cardId; data read from the TanStack Query cache. The feed's onLikeCard becomes "push this route" — one change point covers picture, prompt and sport likes.
  • Hero — always the same 3:4 rounded frame: the liked card itself inside (photo full-bleed, or text-card content top-aligned on panel background, inset below the chip zone, like button omitted), superlike #FF5252 scrim rising from the bottom, Cal Sans title "Envoyer un like à {firstname}" + "Il vous reste 0 Superlikes ❤" overlaid.
  • annuler chip — panel pill, top-right; pressed = opacity .7; tap → router.back(), nothing recorded; Android back does the same; no iOS swipe-dismiss (inherent to fullScreenModal).
  • Message field — single-line, underlined, local state only, discarded on send and cancel (README liberty); keyboard covers the CTAs, "retour" dismisses it. Static layout.
  • Obtenir Superlike — rendered exactly as your mockup (full red text + heart) but inert: disabled + accessibilityState, no pressed feedback. Count comes from SUPERLIKES_REMAINING = 0 in constants.ts.
  • Envoyer Like — white filled pill; tap fires router.back() + likeCard(cardId) together, so the modal slides down onto the already-playing lime overlay, then advance happens under it as today.

Two taste calls I made that you should veto if wrong: hero corner radius 32 (rounder than the feed cards' 24, matching your mockup's poster feel), and adding expo-linear-gradient for the scrim (Expo Go-compatible).

⛭1 action · AskUserQuestion
AskUserQuestion

✳Réflexion

I need to write the UI conventions documentation, but I'm noticing a discrepancy—the docs directory doesn't seem to exist based on the earlier file search, yet CLAUDE.md references docs/brief.md, so the directory might actually be there.

I'll create docs/ui-conventions.md with the key cross-cutting conventions: primary CTAs as full-width pills with specific styling, pressed states for interactive elements at 0.7 opacity, inactive CTAs rendered as designed but disabled, close chips styled as small pills in the top-right of modals, and full-screen modal routes positioned at the top level outside the tabs structure. I'll keep the documentation in English to match the codebase style while the UI copy remains French.

Then I'll invoke the to-issues skill with the mockup reference and conventions context so it can break down the spec into independently-grabbable issues using vertical slices.

✳Réflexion

The to-issues skill is active now, so I'll draft vertical slices and quiz the user with a numbered list at step 4, then iterate until they approve before writing files. I need to quickly check whether .grilled/issues should be tracked—the skill template says .grilled/ must be gitignored, but this project commits .grilled/issues as the tracker with issues 001–011 already committed, and I noticed earlier that .gitignore doesn't actually contain .grilled/.

Since the issues are already committed and tracked, the project convention overrides the generic skill instruction. I'll flag this to the user during the quiz rather than silently gitignoring, since that would contradict the repo's established practice anyway. Now I'm thinking through how to slice this feature—it's small and UI-only, so each slice should be demoable end-to-end. The send-like modal is almost everything in one go, but I can break it into thinner slices: the first slice handles the route, entry point from the like button, and dismissal, with the modal scaffold and annuler chip in place, leaving the content inert for now. Tapping like opens the modal, and cancel or Android back closes it.

The second slice adds the photo hero with the gradient background, 3:4 frame, scrim, title, and heart icon, pulling the superlike count from state—this depends on the first slice being done. The third slice covers text-card heroes with the prompt answer and sport card content in a uniform frame, reusing the frame and scrim from the photo hero. The fourth slice brings in the message field and CTA stack—the "Obtenir Superlike" button stays inert while "Envoyer Like" triggers the send flow, routing back and firing the like action together. This last slice only depends on the first one, not the hero slices, so the bottom compose area can be built independently.

I'll ask the user to confirm the granularity and dependency chain, and I should also flag that the gitignore deviation will stay committed to match the repo convention.

Four thin slices, all AFK, continuing at 012. One deviation from the skill's boilerplate I'm making deliberately: the skill says .grilled/ must be gitignored, but this repo commits .grilled/issues/ as its tracker (issues 001–011 are in history; CLAUDE.md points the implement skill at them) — I'll follow the repo's convention, not the boilerplate.

Proposed breakdown:

  1. 012 — Send-like modal route, entry point & dismissal · AFK · Blocked by: none fullScreenModal route (send-like.tsx thin, screen in the feature) with userId/cardId params; every like button now pushes it instead of calling likeCard; "annuler" chip + Android back dismiss with the feed untouched. Demo: tap any like → dark modal with chip opens over hidden tab bar, cancel → feed unchanged.

  2. 013 — Photo hero with superlike scrim & title · AFK · Blocked by: 012 expo-linear-gradient dep; the 3:4 radius-32 frame showing the liked photo, red scrim, Cal Sans "Envoyer un like à {firstname}", "Il vous reste 0 Superlikes ❤" from SUPERLIKES_REMAINING = 0. Demo: like a photo → mockup screen 1.

  3. 014 — Text-card heroes in the uniform frame · AFK · Blocked by: 013 Prompt-answer and sport-card content rendered top-aligned in the same frame (panel bg, top inset clearing the chip, no like button), same scrim + copy. Demo: mockup screens 2–3.

  4. 015 — Message field, CTA stack & send flow · AFK · Blocked by: 012 (parallel with 013/014) Underlined single-line field (local, discarded), inert red "Obtenir Superlike", white "Envoyer Like" pill firing router.back() + likeCard together so the lime overlay is revealed mid-dismiss. Demo: full happy path ends in the existing like animation + advance.

⛭1 action · Read
Read/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/.grilled/issues/010-reaction-overlay-animations.md
1	title: Reaction overlay — the like & pass animations
2	type: AFK
3	status: done
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Parent
8	
9	[docs/brief.md](../../docs/brief.md) — this is the brief's explicit core deliverable ("Add the pass and like animations").
10	
11	## What to build
12	
13	The full-screen reaction interstitial, exactly as specified by the user during grilling, driven by the store's transient reaction state and built with `react-native-reanimated`:
14	
15	1. On Like or Pass, a **fully opaque** overlay (surface `#0D0D0D`) **fades in ~200ms** over everything (tab bar too if the mockup composition allows; at minimum over the whole feed).
16	2. **Hold 1 second**: centered icon with a slight **scale-pop** on entry (spring) —
17	   - **Pass**: white cross, same glyph as the X button, bigger.
18	   - **Like**: heart filled with primary `#E1FF00`.
19	3. **While opaque**, the profile swap happens invisibly underneath (advance + scroll reset — already built in 009, resequenced to fire mid-overlay).
20	4. **Fade out ~250ms**, revealing the next profile (or the empty state after the last one).
21	
22	Guards: all reaction inputs (hearts, X) **disabled while the sequence runs** — a rapid double-tap must advance exactly one profile. Timings/durations as named constants in `features/feed/constants.ts`.
23	
24	## Acceptance criteria
25	
26	- [ ] Tap heart → fade-in → 1s hold with popping yellow heart → fade-out onto the next profile
27	- [ ] Tap X → same with big white cross
28	- [ ] Profile swap is never visible mid-transition (overlay fully opaque)
29	- [ ] Double-tapping heart/X during the sequence advances exactly one profile
30	- [ ] Last profile's reaction fades out onto the empty state
31	- [ ] Timings (200ms / 1000ms / 250ms) live as constants; `npm run check` green
32	- [ ] Folder `docs` is empty as `brief.md` and `feed-response-example.json` are deleted
33	
34	## Blocked by
35	
36	- `009-feed-store-like-pass.md`
37

Now I'm wrapping up by recapping the full session—the decisions we made, the mockup path we established, the conventions file we created, and the four issues we documented. I'll point to the next step of implementing those issues and mention that the temporary Python server is still running in the background for viewing the mockup, with instructions on how to stop it if needed.

Handoff complete. Here's where everything landed:

Issues written (continuing the repo's committed tracker): - 012-send-like-modal-route.md — fullScreenModal route, like buttons push it, "annuler"/Android-back dismissal - 013-send-like-photo-hero.md — liked photo in the 3:4 frame, #FF5252 scrim, Cal Sans title, SUPERLIKES_REMAINING = 0 - 014-send-like-text-card-heroes.md — prompt/sport cards in the same uniform frame (blocked by 013) - 015-send-like-message-and-ctas.md — message field, inert "Obtenir Superlike", "Envoyer Like" → simultaneous dismiss + likeCard (blocked by 012 only, parallel with 013/014)

Each one references the approved mockup, cites the relevant docs/ui-conventions.md rules, and is stamped "UI-only — no server, schema, or business-logic changes". All AFK.

Supporting artifacts: - send-like-v1.html — the approved mockup (4 phone variants + states/destinations table). Still viewable in the Browser pane via the temp server on port 8788; kill the background python3 http.server whenever you're done with it. - docs/ui-conventions.md — new, committed: primary pill CTA recipe, pressed = opacity 0.7, inert out-of-scope CTAs, fullScreenModal + dismiss-chip pattern, dark-only theme. This re-seeds the docs/ folder that issue 010 emptied.

One deliberate deviation from the to-issues boilerplate: I did not gitignore .grilled/ — this repo commits its issues as the tracker (001–011 are in history), so the new ones follow suit.

Next step when you're ready: /implement-next-issue will pick up 012.

✳Réflexion

I'm planning the implementation for task 012, which I'm familiar with from earlier work. I need to create a new route file that re-exports the SendLikeScreen component and register it in the root layout with the appropriate navigation stack options.

For the modal presentation, I'm deciding between setting the options directly in the route file versus declaring them in the root layout. The idiomatic approach in expo-router is to configure per-screen options in the layout file using a Stack.Screen entry, which aligns with keeping route files thin. I'll set presentation: 'fullScreenModal' and animation: 'slide_from_bottom' — the animation is default on iOS for modals but needs to be explicit on Android.

Now for the send-like screen component, I'm extracting the userId and cardId from route params and pulling the feed data from the cache using the same query hook. The issue is that for this first iteration, I should keep it minimal — just render the dark surface background with the annuler chip that dismisses the screen, without resolving or displaying the profile and card data yet since that comes in later iterations. I need to be careful not to resolve data I'm not using, as that would trigger linting errors about unused variables. Android hardware back will automatically pop the route since expo-router handles it natively for stack screens, and fullScreenModal on iOS has no swipe gesture by default so that's fine. I'm updating the feed-screen to push the send-like route with userId and cardId params instead of calling likeCard directly, which 015 will reintroduce when actually processing the like.

Removing likeCard from feed-screen won't cause knip issues since it's a store property, not an exported function, and the store tests still use it anyway. I'm adding a CANCEL_LABEL constant set to 'annuler' in constants.ts for the French label, keeping accessibility labels minimal. The status bar is already global and the screen uses safe area insets for top padding so the chip sits properly below it.

For the chip positioning, I'm placing it absolutely in the top-right corner using the safe area insets plus some offset — around insets.top + 20 for vertical placement and 28 pixels from the right edge to account for the hero's margins and the chip's own spacing.

Building the component structure with a container View and a Pressable chip element... The cancel chip mockup uses a white text label at 15 semibold weight with 10x20 padding, rounded corners, and a minimum height of 40 for proper touch targets. I'm setting up the route for the send-like screen as a full-screen modal that slides up from the bottom, converting the self-closing Stack to include this child screen. I'll verify the imports are correct and remove the now-unused likeCard reference from the feed screen.

Since the modal handles recording the like reaction going forward, the feed screen just needs to pass through the profile, advance, restart, and reaction props — the onLikeCard closure captures the current user ID so the modal can properly associate the like with the sender. I'm checking whether typed routes are available through the tsconfig setup. The route file and constants follow the same pattern as existing routes, so knip should handle them fine with the expo-router plugin awareness. Now I need to check the tsconfig and app.json to see if typed routes are enabled and understand how that affects the type generation.

✳Réflexion

I found that Issue 012 is the lowest-priority unblocked item, so I'm starting work on it now on the superlike-page branch.

⛭2 actions · mcp__ccd_session__mark_chapter, Bash
mcp__ccd_session__mark_chapterIssue 012: modal route & dismissal
