Bashcat /Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/tsconfig.json /Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/app.json
{
  "extends": "expo/tsconfig.base",
  "compilerOptions": {
    "strict": true,
    "paths": {
      "@/*": ["./src/*"]
    }
  },
  "include": ["**/*.ts", "**/*.tsx", ".expo/types/**/*.ts", "expo-env.d.ts"]
}
{
  "expo": {
    "name": "bpm",
    "slug": "bpm",
    "version": "1.0.0",
    "orientation": "portrait",
    "icon": "./assets/images/icon.png",
    "scheme": "bpm",
    "userInterfaceStyle": "dark",
    "backgroundColor": "#0D0D0D",
    "ios": {
      "icon": "./assets/expo.icon"
    },
    "android": {
      "adaptiveIcon": {
        "backgroundColor": "#0D0D0D",
        "foregroundImage": "./assets/images/android-icon-foreground.png",
        "backgroundImage": "./assets/images/android-icon-background.png",
        "monochromeImage": "./assets/images/android-icon-monochrome.png"
      },
      "predictiveBackGestureEnabled": false
    },
    "web": {
      "output": "static",
      "favicon": "./assets/images/favicon.png"
    },
    "plugins": [
      "expo-router",
      [
        "expo-splash-screen",
        {
          "backgroundColor": "#0D0D0D"
        }
      ]
    ],
    "experiments": {
      "typedRoutes": true,
      "reactCompiler": true
    }
  }
}
✳Réflexion

Typed routes are enabled and the tsconfig includes the expo types directory, so I need to regenerate the route types after adding the new route or the type checker will fail. I'm trying to figure out the right command to regenerate those types—it should happen automatically with expo start or npx expo export, but I'm not entirely sure about the exact process in SDK 50.

Let me check the current .expo/types/router.d.ts file to see its format, then I can either manually update it with the new route or regenerate it properly. Since the file is gitignored and gets overwritten on the next expo start anyway, I could manually add the '/send-like' route to the union type as a quick fix, but it's better to understand the proper regeneration flow. For the typed routes to work with router.push, the pathname needs to be a known route in the type definitions.

✳Réflexion

I see that typedRoutes is enabled and the tsconfig includes .expo/types, so I need to check the generated route types file to ensure it picks up the new route.

⛭1 action · Bash
Bashls /Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/.expo/types/ 2>/dev/null && cat /Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/.expo/types/router.d.ts 2>/dev/null | head -40
router.d.ts
/* eslint-disable */
import * as Router from 'expo-router';

export * from 'expo-router';

declare module 'expo-router' {
  export namespace ExpoRouter {
    export interface __routes<T extends string | object = string> {
      hrefInputParams: { pathname: Router.RelativePathString, params?: Router.UnknownInputParams } | { pathname: Router.ExternalPathString, params?: Router.UnknownInputParams } | { pathname: `/boost`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `/filters`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `/rewind`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `/_sitemap`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}` | `/`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}/likes` | `/likes`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}/matchs` | `/matchs`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}/profil` | `/profil`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; };
      hrefOutputParams: { pathname: Router.RelativePathString, params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams } | { pathname: Router.ExternalPathString, params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams } | { pathname: `/boost`; params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams; } | { pathname: `/filters`; params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams; } | { pathname: `/rewind`; params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams; } | { pathname: `/_sitemap`; params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}` | `/`; params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}/likes` | `/likes`; params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}/matchs` | `/matchs`; params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}/profil` | `/profil`; params?: Router.UnknownOutputParams; };
      href: Router.RelativePathString | Router.ExternalPathString | `/boost${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `/filters${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `/rewind${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `/_sitemap${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `${'/(tabs)'}${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `/${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `${'/(tabs)'}/likes${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `/likes${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `${'/(tabs)'}/matchs${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `/matchs${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `${'/(tabs)'}/profil${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | `/profil${`?${string}` | `#${string}` | ''}` | { pathname: Router.RelativePathString, params?: Router.UnknownInputParams } | { pathname: Router.ExternalPathString, params?: Router.UnknownInputParams } | { pathname: `/boost`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `/filters`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `/rewind`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `/_sitemap`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}` | `/`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}/likes` | `/likes`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}/matchs` | `/matchs`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; } | { pathname: `${'/(tabs)'}/profil` | `/profil`; params?: Router.UnknownInputParams; };
    }
  }
}
✳Réflexion

I need to figure out how to regenerate the types after adding the new file. The npx expo customize command is for templates, not type generation. Looking at the available options, I could run npx expo start briefly in the background to trigger the generation process, though that's interactive and long-running.

The cleaner approach is to write the new route file and the updated layout configuration, then kick off a short-lived expo start session to regenerate the types. I'm creating the send-like route component and adding its corresponding screen entry to the navigation stack.

Adding route files... Writing component styles...

For iOS fullscreen modals the status bar is already hidden, so that's fine. The Stack's contentStyle sets the surface background globally, but I'll keep the container background explicit for consistency with how the feed screen handles it. Now I need to add the cancel label constant to the constants file — "annuler" in lowercase per the mockup — and I'm debating whether to call it SEND_LIKE_CANCEL_LABEL to match the naming pattern of LIKE_LABEL and PASS_LABEL, or just CANCEL_LABEL since "annuler" is specific to this modal.

I'll go with CANCEL_LABEL for simplicity. Then in the feed screen component, I'm removing the likeCard selector and importing the router instead, so when a card is liked it navigates to the send-like modal with the card ID and user ID as route parameters.

The overlay will prevent accidental taps during the reaction sequence, so there's no issue with the guard logic. The store's likeCard method isn't used anywhere in the source code now—it only appears in tests—but that's fine since knip only flags unused exports and files, not individual store methods. I should make sure the prop naming stays consistent and then run the checks to catch any other issues biome might flag.

Looking at the existing code style, JSX props are sorted alphabetically—like in the Pressable component with accessibilityLabel, accessibilityRole, onPress, style. I'll keep ProfileCardList's props in that same order: cards, key, onLikeCard. For Stack.Screen, name comes before options alphabetically. Now I need to regenerate the typed routes.

I'm thinking through how to run npx expo start to generate the router types file—it needs a TTY and will create .expo/types/router.d.ts. I'll start it in the background with a timeout, then poll for the 'send-like' route in the generated file before killing the process.

Route types will need regeneration after I add the route (typed routes are on). Writing the code first:

I need to regenerate the typed routes by spinning up the Expo development server briefly to generate the router type definitions, then shut it down and start polling for completion.

Now regenerate the typed-routes declaration — a brief headless expo start writes it:

⛭1 action · Bash
Bash(CI=1 npx expo start --port 8090 > /dev/null 2>&1 &); for i in $(seq 1 30); do grep -q 'send-like' .expo/types/router.d.ts 2>/dev/null && break; /bin/sleep 1; done; grep -o 'send-like' .expo/types/router.d.ts | head -1; pkill -f 'expo start --port 8090' || true
send-like

Now I need to test the dismissal flow: clicking the annuler chip should close the modal and return to the untouched feed showing the same profile (Camille) at the same scroll position, and the store should remain unchanged with likedCardIds still empty. Since the store isn't exposed globally, I'll verify this through UI observation—no reaction overlay should play and the feed should be exactly as it was before opening the modal.

The dismissal action is working correctly—I'm back on the feed with Camille still visible, and no reaction overlay appeared. The feed itself wasn't affected, so no like was recorded and the view didn't advance. I'm noticing the scroll position shifted slightly between screenshots, with the photo starting a bit higher than before.

This is likely from my earlier stray clicks that may have caused a small scroll rather than a defect. The keyed remount only happens when the profile changes, so back navigation preserves the ScrollView state. Now I want to verify that clicking the like button on a prompt card (the second Liker ref) also opens the modal, then do one final check to confirm the single change point handles text cards properly.

Dismissal works — back on the untouched feed, same profile, no reaction played, nothing recorded. One more check: the prompt card's internal like button flows through the same entry point:

The file /Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/.grilled/issues/012-send-like-modal-route.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)
✳Réflexion

I've implemented the issue with a new thin route for sending likes and a dedicated screen component, updated the feed to trigger that route, added the cancel label constant, and modified the layout file. The changes span five files total, and I'm not committing yet—you'll want to run the tests yourself and verify everything works as expected before doing that.

The modal is set up as a full-screen presentation, though on web it'll just show as regular navigation instead of the native slide-from-bottom animation. All lint, type checks, and tests pass cleanly, and I've verified in the browser that the like action opens the send-like screen with the right parameters, hides the tab bar, and the cancel/back buttons properly restore the feed. Issue 012 is marked done, which unblocks 015, though 013 and 014 are still pending—013 is next and unblocked, while 014 depends on 013. I'd recommend testing on Expo Go to see the actual native modal presentation.

Implemented: 012 — Send-like modal — route, entry point & dismissal ✅

Files changed: - send-like.tsx — new thin route (re-exports the feature screen) - send-like-screen.tsx — new modal shell: surface background, "annuler" chip (panel pill, pressed = opacity 0.7, accessibility-labelled) calling router.back() - _layout.tsx — send-like registered as fullScreenModal + slide-from-bottom on the root stack - feed-screen.tsx — the like callback now pushes /send-like with cardId + userId params instead of calling likeCard; nothing touches the store on open - constants.ts — CANCEL_LABEL = 'annuler' - 012-send-like-modal-route.md — marked done - (.expo/types/router.d.ts regenerated for typed routes — gitignored)

Checks: npm run check fully green — biome clean, tsc --noEmit clean, vitest 19/19 passed, knip clean.

Verified live (Expo web, mobile viewport): liking the photo card and the prompt-answer card both open /send-like with the correct per-card cardId, tab bar hidden, dark shell with the chip top-right; "annuler" returns to the untouched feed — same profile, no reaction overlay, nothing recorded. One caveat: the slide-from-bottom animation is native-only, so its feel is worth a glance in Expo Go.

Please run the tests yourself (npm run check) to confirm before moving on. I haven't committed anything — when you're ready to capture this, use the /commit-no-push skill.

Remaining: 3 todo issues — 013 (photo hero, now unblocked and next in line), 014 (text-card heroes, blocked by 013), 015 (message + CTAs, unblocked now that 012 is done). Run /implement-next-issue again to take 013.

H
Hugo
/commit-no-push
✳
Claude

I need to run the checks first, then commit the tracked files that have changes. The instruction is specifically about adding tracked files with changes—new files like the send-like components and documentation aren't tracked yet, so they wouldn't be included in this commit. I'm thinking through whether to use git add -u to stage only the modified tracked files.

✳Réflexion

All the checks are passing—lint and type checking ran as part of the initial check step, and the tests plus knip validation completed successfully since the script is chained with &&. Looking at git status, I've got three modified tracked files and some new untracked files related to this work (issues, mockups, the UI conventions doc, and the two new source files), while .claude/launch.json remains unchanged. The repo follows a convention of committing grilled issues.

The skill guide says to add all tracked files with changes, but leaving out the new source files would break the commit since the feed-screen route wouldn't exist without them. These new files are intentional deliverables, so I'll stage everything together—tracked changes plus the new files—which aligns with how the repo has handled issues before. For the commit message, I'm going with a conventional format: feat(feed): add send-like modal route with entry point and dismissal as the subject line, then a short body mentioning the grilled artifacts (issues 012-015, mockup, ui-conventions docs) and a co-author line per the harness instructions.

✳Réflexion

All checks passed—the untracked files are intentional outputs from this session, and I'm committing everything including the new route/screen files since the repo convention requires .grilled/ and excluding them would break the build.

⛭1 action · Bash
Bashgit add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF' feat(feed): add send-like modal route with entry point and dismissal Issue 012: liking any Likable Card now opens a full-screen modal (expo-router fullScreenModal, tab bar hidden) before anything is recorded; the "annuler" chip and Android back dismiss it leaving the feed untouched. Recording the like moves to the modal in issue 015. Also ships the grilling artifacts: issues 012-015, the approved send-like mockup, and docs/ui-conventions.md. Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com> EOF )" && git log --oneline -1 && git status --porcelain

71375fe feat(feed): add send-like modal route with entry point and dismissal

Committed as 71375fe on superlike-page — feat(feed): add send-like modal route with entry point and dismissal, 11 files (the 3 modified source files, the 2 new ones, issues 012–015, the approved mockup, and docs/ui-conventions.md). Working tree is clean, nothing pushed.

Before the commit I re-ran npm run check: biome, typecheck, vitest (19/19) and knip all green.

Next up whenever you're ready: /implement-next-issue will take 013 (photo hero with the superlike scrim).

H
Hugo
/implement-next-issue
arguments
-
✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Now I can move forward with issue 013 since 012 is complete. I need to build a photo hero component with a 3:4 aspect ratio, rounded corners, and horizontal margins, displaying the image full-bleed with a thumbhash placeholder. The key addition is a scrim—a gradient overlay that fades from transparent at the top to the superlike color at the bottom, which requires adding a new color dependency.

The overlay text goes bottom-left with the user's first name in a Cal Sans title and a remaining superlikes count with a filled heart icon, all in the fill color. I'll need to add the SUPERLIKES_REMAINING constant and related copy to the constants file, and the "annuler" chip sits in the top-right corner. The implementation starts by installing the gradient library, then resolving the profile and card data from route parameters and cache.

Resolving the profile and card...

For picture cards, the hero displays the photo; text cards come later in phase 014, so for now the frame just shows the scrim and title over an empty background. If the profile is missing—like from a bad deep link or empty cache—I'll render a minimal shell with just the chip, since the title needs a firstname to display properly. In practice with typed navigation flows, the profile should always be present, but I'll add a guard just in case.

I'm extracting the hero into its own component called SendLikeHero that takes the card and firstname as props, keeping the screen file lean. The component renders the frame with the photo for picture cards, applies the gradient scrim, and displays the copy—ready to extend for text cards later.

Writing component structure... I'm debating whether to use a function constant like SEND_LIKE_TITLE that takes firstname as a parameter, or follow the house style of keeping constants as static data and doing the interpolation in the component itself. Looking at how RELATIONSHIP_TYPE_COPY is structured as a record and checking FeedHeader for precedent on how it handles firstname assembly, it seems the pattern leans toward keeping constants as plain strings and composing them in the component. Defining color constants... The chip positioning works out—it sits 16 pixels from the hero's top edge, which aligns with the inset calculations. For the copy block at the bottom, I'm setting the title in Cal Sans at 33 with tight line height, and the count row uses flexbox with a heart icon and reduced opacity text below it.

Now I'm setting up the SendLikeScreen component, which pulls the card and profile data from the feed, applies the safe area insets to the container padding, and renders the hero with the card details.

The chip component stays positioned absolutely with a higher z-index to overlay the hero, and I'm using the same inset calculation for its top position. The typed route params should work fine with strict mode since I'm declaring the expected shape explicitly.

For the image, I'll reuse the same pattern from PictureCard with the thumbhash placeholder and a 300ms transition. The heart icon comes from lucide-react-native at size 18, and I'm applying a slight opacity to the count row. I need to install the dependencies first before finishing this out.

Now I'm mapping out the files I need to create and modify: the new send-like-hero component, updates to send-like-screen, adding constants for the superlikes UI, extending the colors file, and updating the package dependencies. After implementation, I'll restart the preview server since adding expo-linear-gradient requires a metro rebuild.

Before I touch colors.ts, I should check what the existing color tests validate to make sure I'm following the same pattern.

Next available issue: 013 — photo hero with superlike scrim & title (blocked only by 012, now done). Starting with the one file that has a test guarding it — the palette — since I need a transparent variant of the superlike red for the gradient:

1
✳Réflexion

I'm looking at the regex pattern for validating colors — it requires the alpha value in rgba to match 0.\d+, which means rgba(255, 82, 82, 0) would fail since "0" doesn't have a decimal point. Using 0.01 as a workaround feels hacky, so I should consider extending the regex to handle this case better.

The real issue is that the test file is technically read-only for the proof, but modifying the regex to allow optional decimals with 0(\.\d+)? is a legitimate fix. Alternatively, I could use the 'transparent' keyword in the LinearGradient, but that's not a hex literal and violates the COLORS rule. Plus, 'transparent' is really rgba(0,0,0,0) — a transparent black — which would create a muddy dark band when fading to red since expo-linear-gradient doesn't use premultiplied interpolation like modern browsers do.

The solution is to add a dedicated transparent red color to the palette: superlikeTransparent: 'rgba(255, 82, 82, 0)'. I'll update the test regex to accept alpha values of 0 or 0.x, then add the new color to colors.ts, widen the test pattern, and set up the constants for the like feature including the title prefix and labels.

Now I'm creating the send-like-hero component... Writing component markup... Finishing the stylesheet definitions...

The LinearGradient colors prop needs to match the tuple type from expo-linear-gradient, and while TypeScript infers inline array literals as string[], contextual typing against the tuple type makes it work without needing an explicit as const assertion. For the aspect ratio, the photo mockup frame at 492 height by 369 width fits the layout constraints on smaller screens like the 812pt height device, with the hero section, message, and CTAs all stacking within the available space. Now moving on to update the send-like-screen component.

I'm extracting the route params for cardId and userId, then querying the feed data to find the matching profile and liked card, and rendering the hero section with the chip positioned correctly at insets.top + 20.

Now I need to update the comment about the message field and CTAs landing in a later phase, check the query client caching configuration, then install the new dependency and restart the preview server to pick up the changes.

The palette test's regex only allows 0.\d+ alphas, so the transparent gradient stop needs a small test widening too. First, the new dependency:

  npm audit fix --force

~57.0.0

✳Réflexion

Looking at React Native versioning and the changes to StyleSheet APIs — in newer versions, absoluteFillObject might be deprecated or renamed, so I should use explicit positioning with position: 'absolute' and inset: 0, or check if absoluteFill is still available as a registered style reference.

✳Réflexion

RN 0.86 removed absoluteFillObject, so I'll write out the offsets explicitly like reaction-overlay.tsx does.

Everything's building successfully. I need to restart the Metro dev server since it was running before the new gradient module was installed, then test the flow: navigate to a feed, like a photo, and verify the hero section displays correctly with the photo, red overlay, like button text, remaining superlikes count, and the chip. I should also confirm that liking a different photo card updates to show that image instead of the first one.

Checks green. Restarting the dev server so Metro picks up the new dependency, then verifying visually:

✳Réflexion

The second picture card is rendering a different photo, which confirms that criterion. The thumbhash placeholder is working as expected — the fresh navigation triggered a new fetch and the image loaded from the blur effect, matching what I saw in the feed screenshots. The hero component uses the same image props, so the thumbhash behavior is consistent, though I did notice on a full page reload that the photo loads with some delay.

Now I'll navigate back to the feed to clean up the state, mark this test as complete, and report the results.

Different card, different photo — the hero tracks the liked card. Marking 013 done and returning the preview to the feed:

The file /Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/.grilled/issues/013-send-like-photo-hero.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

Implemented: 013 — Send-like modal — photo hero with superlike scrim & title ✅

Files changed: - send-like-hero.tsx — new: the uniform 3:4 / radius-32 frame rendering the liked photo full-bleed (expo-image + thumbhash, same recipe as the feed's picture card), the superlike scrim, and the Cal Sans title + "Il vous reste 0 Superlikes ❤" block - send-like-screen.tsx — resolves userId/cardId params against the TanStack Query cache and mounts the hero under the chip - constants.ts — SUPERLIKES_REMAINING = 0 (README-liberty note, next to the other static viewer data), SEND_LIKE_TITLE_PREFIX, SUPERLIKES_REMAINING_LABEL - colors.ts — superlikeTransparent: rgba(255, 82, 82, 0), the gradient's top stop (plain 'transparent' fades through black with expo-linear-gradient); colors.test.ts regex widened to accept a zero alpha - package.json — expo-linear-gradient@~57.0.0 via npx expo install (Expo Go-compatible)

Checks: npm run check green — biome clean, typecheck clean (after swapping StyleSheet.absoluteFillObject, which RN 0.86's strict types dropped, for explicit offsets), vitest 19/19, knip clean.

Verified live (Expo web): liking Camille's first photo shows that photo with the red scrim, Cal Sans "Envoyer un like à Camille" and the count line — visually matching the approved mockup; opening the second picture card's cardId shows the other photo, proving the hero tracks the liked card. Gradient blend looks clean (no muddy band).

Please run npm run check yourself to confirm, and use /commit-no-push when you want this captured — I haven't committed.

Remaining: 2 todo — 014 (text-card heroes, now unblocked, next in line) and 015 (message + CTAs, unblocked). Run /implement-next-issue again to take 014.

H
Hugo
/commit-no-push

faed520 feat(feed): add send-like photo hero with superlike scrim

Committed as faed520 — feat(feed): add send-like photo hero with superlike scrim, 8 files. All checks re-ran green before the commit (biome, typecheck, vitest 19/19, knip). Working tree clean, nothing pushed.

Next: /implement-next-issue will take 014 (text-card heroes in the uniform frame).

H
Hugo
/implement-next-issue
✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Now I'm looking at the next card component to build—the text-card heroes, which was blocked until the previous task finished. The design needs a quote badge with uppercase muted prompt text and a Cal Sans answer, positioned at the top of a panel with about 76 pixels of inset to clear the chip area, and longer answers should flow under a scrim. The sport card variant uses rings and a "Séances / semaine" list with the same top alignment and panel styling. Neither card includes a like button.

To avoid duplicating code between the existing card components and the new hero versions, I'm extracting the content portions—PromptAnswerBody and SportCardBody—so both the full cards (which have the panel wrapper, border, and like row) and the heroes (which use the frame's background and radius) can reuse the same internals. This keeps the visual styling consistent without nesting conflicting border radius or panel wrappers.

In the hero component, I'm rendering a switch that checks the card type and wraps each body component in a styled view container. The border approach makes sense — keeping it consistent with photo cards in the feed, so no border on the frame. For the locked picture and info card in the hero, they just render nothing when not likable, which is fine. The chip positioning checks out: with the top inset plus padding, it lands around 76 pixels down, giving proper breathing room from the hero content.

Now I'm setting up the sport-card-body component to handle the activity rings. I need to pull in the ActivityRings component and wire up the training frequency constants that define the ring colors and sweep values. Completing the SportCardBody component with activity rings and a list of training frequency rows, then refactoring sport-card.tsx to import and use SportCardBody while keeping the like button and panel styling separate.

Now I'm working on the prompt-answer-body component, which will handle the shared layout for displaying prompt answers across different card contexts. The panel structure keeps the 20-unit gap between body and likeRow while the body itself has a 20-unit gap between header and answer, which renders identically. PromptHeader is a separate shared component used for picture prompts with different styling (16 semibold white versus the 11 uppercase muted style here), so I'm keeping them distinct. Now I'm updating the hero section to conditionally render different content based on card type—images for picture cards, the PromptAnswerBody component wrapped in a text container for prompt answers, and similar handling for sport cards.

Adding conditional rendering...

Next available: 014 — text-card heroes in the uniform frame (blocker 013 is done). The issue leaves the reuse mechanism to me: I'll extract the card bodies (content without panel/like button) into shared components so the feed cards and the hero render the exact same internals. Three edits + two new files:

The prompt-answer hero is matching the mockup with the quote badge, uppercase title in Cal Sans, panel background, red scrim, and title overlay positioned correctly. I'm noticing the "annuler" chip has lost its visible panel-pill background styling — it's rendering as plain text because the chip background color matches the frame background, making it invisible. This worked fine against the photo hero where there was contrast, but against the text card it's becoming an issue.

Looking back at the mockup variants, the chip actually appears the same way there too — plain text without a visible pill in variant 2 and 3. The approved design has this same behavior, so it's faithful to what was signed off on.

Now moving on to the sport card hero.

✳Réflexion

The prompt hero matches mockup screen 2 with the chip pill blending into the panel background as approved. Now I'll check the sport card.

The file /Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/.grilled/issues/014-send-like-text-card-heroes.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

Implemented: 014 — Send-like modal — text-card heroes in the uniform frame ✅

Approach: extracted the card bodies into shared components so the feed cards and the hero render the exact same internals — no duplication, no drift.

Files changed: - prompt-answer-body.tsx — new: quote badge + uppercase title + Cal Sans answer, extracted from the feed card - sport-card-body.tsx — new: activity rings + "Séances / semaine" list with dotted leaders, extracted likewise - prompt-answer-card.tsx / sport-card.tsx — slimmed to panel + body + like row (styles unchanged) - send-like-hero.tsx — a card-type switch: photos full-bleed as before; prompt/sport bodies top-aligned with a 76px top inset clearing the "annuler" chip; info/locked unreachable → empty frame. Scrim + title block untouched, so their position is shared by construction.

Checks: npm run check green — biome, typecheck, vitest 19/19, knip.

Verified live (Expo web, mobile viewport): the prompt-answer hero and the sport-card hero both match mockup screens 2–3 — content clear of the chip, no like button, same scrim/title composition as the photo hero; and the feed's prompt + sport cards render exactly as before the refactor.

Please run npm run check to confirm, and /commit-no-push when you want it captured — I haven't committed.

Remaining: 1 todo — 015 (message field, CTA stack & send flow), unblocked. Run /implement-next-issue to take it.

Committed as ee8e8a5 — feat(feed): render liked text cards in the send-like hero, 6 files. Checks re-ran green before committing (biome, typecheck, vitest 19/19, knip). Working tree clean, nothing pushed.

Hugo
/implement-next-issue
✳
Claude
✳Réflexion

Issue 015 is the last remaining todo, unblocked now that 012 is complete. I need to build out the message field with a text input that sits below the hero, styled with a muted placeholder and a subtle bottom border, plus a pinned CTA stack at the bottom with 20px margins. The message input will be local state that gets discarded, and the keyboard can cover the CTAs since the return key dismisses it anyway.

For the CTAs themselves, I'm creating two buttons: a disabled "Obtenir Superlike" button styled as a text button in superlike red that's inert and reports its disabled state, and an "Envoyer Like" primary pill button that's fully rounded with a pressed opacity of 0.7. The send flow triggers router.back() and likeCard() simultaneously, with a store guard preventing double-taps from creating multiple likes.

Adding the French copy to constants.ts... Writing component styles... Restructuring the JSX to guard the entire content block with the profile and likedCard check, keeping only the chip outside — this way handleSend safely accesses likedCard.id through closure since it's defined within the guarded scope. For the keyboard, I'm keeping the TextInput single-line with a send action on return.

Now I'm thinking through the double-tap risk — if the user taps send twice before the route animation completes, router.back() fires twice and could pop the underlying tabs screen. I'll add a ref flag to prevent multiple submissions.

The layout is tight at around 810 pixels total height against an 812 viewport, but that matches the mockup design exactly. On smaller phones it might overflow, though that's an accepted constraint. I also need to make sure the chip renders above the content with proper z-index.

Now I'm implementing the send-like screen component, importing the necessary dependencies and setting up the state management with the feed store. Setting up the SendLikeScreen component with router navigation, safe area insets, and extracting the card and user IDs from route params, then fetching the liked card data from the feed store to display in the modal. Writing component markup... Adding styling details... The import order will get sorted automatically when I run the linter, so I'll just write the code and let that handle it. Then I need to test the flow: type a message, hit send, verify the like advances to the next person with the overlay effect, and confirm the feed updates correctly. Once that works in the browser, I'm done.

Only 015 left — message field, CTA stack & send flow — and its blocker (012) is done. Adding the copy constants first, then the screen's lower half:

The screenshot shows the modal dismissed with the lime reaction overlay mid-animation, displaying the yellow heart pop over the surface, and the URL reset to /. The send flow is working as expected—dismissing the modal and liking the card triggers the overlay animation while the feed advances underneath. I'm waiting for the fade-out to complete so I can verify the feed moved to the next profile (Thomas) and confirm the like was recorded exactly once.

Caught the money shot — the lime heart overlay playing right as the modal cleared. Waiting for the fade-out to confirm the advance:

✳Réflexion

I'm tracking down a timing issue where the overlay animation seems to be running longer than expected—around 1.45 seconds total with the hold and fade. I'm wondering if the FeedScreen is remounting when the modal closes, which could cause the useEffect to fire again and restart the animation sequence, especially on web with expo-router's back navigation behavior.

Heart still holding — one more beat to see it resolve:

- Executed on tabId: tab-2

  • tabId tab-2: "localhost:8081" (http://localhost:8081)
✳Réflexion

The full happy path is working as expected — I went through the complete flow of opening the modal, typing a message, sending the like, dismissing it, and watching the overlay fade out before the feed advanced to the next profile. The overlay animation took slightly longer than the standard 1.45 seconds on web due to a remount restart, but it resolved correctly with exactly one advance, which matches the spec since the native feed stays mounted under the modal. This isn't a defect — one like resulted in one advance as required.

Now I need to verify the last criterion: that the message gets discarded when the modal closes. I'll reopen the like on Thomas to confirm the message field is empty.

The overlay faded out onto Thomas — exactly one advance, one like. Last criterion: the message is gone on a fresh open:

The modal resets properly with an empty input field and placeholder text restored after dismissing the message. I'm verifying that all acceptance criteria pass: sending a message dismisses the overlay and advances the state with the entry recorded once, and the double-tap interaction includes the necessary reference and store guard at the code level.

The Superlike button displays correctly as disabled, messages are properly cleared on both send and cancel through unmounting, the return key dismisses the keyboard as expected, and the French copy is in place. All four issues are now complete, so I'll mark this done and suggest committing the changes, then optionally running a final verification on Expo Go before wrapping up.

Empty field, placeholder restored — the message died with the modal. Cleaning up and marking 015 done:

Edit/Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/.grilled/issues/015-send-like-message-and-ctas.md
The file /Users/hugobayoud/prog/bpm-test-technique/.grilled/issues/015-send-like-message-and-ctas.md has been updated successfully. (file state is current in your context — no need to Read it back)

Implemented: 015 — Send-like modal — message field, CTA stack & send flow ✅ — and with it, the send-like feature is complete: all 15 issues in the tracker are done.

Files changed: - send-like-screen.tsx — the lower half: underlined single-line TextInput (local state, returnKeyType="done"), the inert red "Obtenir Superlike ❤" (disabled + accessibilityState), and the white "Envoyer Like" pill (58px, pressed = opacity 0.7) whose handler fires likeCard(cardId) + router.back() together, with a one-shot ref so a double-tap can't pop the navigator twice (the store's existing guard already caps it at one recorded like) - constants.ts — MESSAGE_PLACEHOLDER, GET_SUPERLIKE_LABEL, SEND_LIKE_LABEL

Checks: npm run check green — biome, typecheck, vitest 19/19, knip.

Verified live (Expo web): the complete screen matches your mockup; "Obtenir Superlike" reports aria-disabled="true" and clicking it does nothing; typing a message then "Envoyer Like" dismissed the modal onto the lime heart overlay mid-animation (screenshot caught it), which faded out onto Thomas — exactly one like, one advance; reopening the modal on Thomas showed an empty field. One web-only observation: the overlay held slightly longer than the native 1.45s sequence because the web navigator remounts the feed on back — on Expo Go the feed stays mounted under the modal, so the timing there is the spec'd one; worth a quick device check.

Please run npm run check to confirm, then /commit-no-push to capture it — I haven't committed.

Remaining: 0 todo issues. Nothing blocked, nothing HITL. The natural next steps: commit, give the flow a feel on Expo Go (modal slide + overlay timing), and optionally a /review pass over the superlike-page branch before opening a PR.

H
Hugo
/commit-no-push
✳
Claude

Bashgit add -A && git commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF' feat(feed): complete send-like modal with message field and send flow Issue 015: optional message input (local state, discarded on send and cancel — README liberty), inert "Obtenir Superlike" text button (0-Superlikes scope, disabled for accessibility) and the primary "Envoyer Like" pill. Sending fires likeCard and router.back together so the modal dismisses onto the reaction overlay already playing; a one-shot guard keeps a double-tap to a single like and pop. Everything's committed and ready to go.

Committed as 14580d1 — feat(feed): complete send-like modal with message field and send flow, 3 files. Checks re-ran green first (biome, typecheck, vitest 19/19, knip). Working tree clean, nothing pushed.

That closes out the feature — the superlike-page branch now carries four commits (012 → 015), every issue in .grilled/issues/ is done, and the flow is verified end-to-end on Expo web. When you're ready: a quick feel-check on Expo Go (modal slide-up + overlay timing are native-only), then push and open the PR whenever you like.
